ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for bebek penyet. FOODON_00002372 (duck food product) is the closest parent class covering duck-based prepared dishes.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers cooked duck meat as the closest parent. No specific composite code exists for bebek penyet with rice and sambal.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for bebek penyet or smashed duck dish. Leaving null per ontology honesty policy.

allergens: none identified (soy sauce variants may contain soy and gluten)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising roughly 150 g of deep-fried smashed duck, 200 g of steamed white rice, 50 g of sambal, and 50 g of fresh vegetable sides such as cucumber and tomato.

nutrient_source.title: Bebek Goreng (Fried Duck) and Steamed White Rice — composite estimate from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for bebek penyet with rice as a composite dish. Values are estimated by weighted combination: approximately 33% fried duck (FAO/INFOODS SEA fried duck values; USDA SR Legacy duck meat cooked roasted NDB 05141 and fried poultry analogues) and 44% steamed white rice (USDA SR Legacy, cooked white rice, NDB 20045), with 11% sambal and 11% vegetable sides. Per-100g values reflect the blended plate composition. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — white rice (boiled) and mixed rice-based meals
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bebek penyet with rice. GI estimated at 68 based on the dominant carbohydrate component being steamed white rice (GI ~72 per University of Sydney GI Database for boiled white rice, Southeast Asian variety). The presence of duck fat, sambal, and vegetable sides moderates the overall GI slightly below that of plain white rice. Analogous estimate anchored to University of Sydney GI Database white rice entry and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (68 × 101.25) / 100 ≈ 27.0 g GL per 450 g serving (carbohydrate_g per serving = 22.5 × 4.5 = 101.25 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from sambal, seasoning, and frying process
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit bebek penyet with rice to occasional consumption, as a single 450 g serving provides approximately 1395 mg sodium (nearly 60% of the 2300 mg daily limit), primarily from sambal paste, marinating salts, and frying seasonings; requesting reduced sambal and avoiding added soy sauce can meaningfully lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from white rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the white rice component contributes a glycemic load of approximately 27 per serving (GI ~68), which can cause a moderate postprandial glucose rise; portion control of the rice, pairing with the duck protein and fat to slow gastric emptying, and substituting brown rice where available are recommended strategies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from deep-fried duck skin and cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease, bebek penyet with rice presents a moderate concern due to approximately 8.6 g saturated fat and 248 mg cholesterol per 450 g serving from the deep-fried duck; removing the skin before eating and limiting portion frequency to once weekly can help manage saturated fat and dietary cholesterol intake within heart-healthy guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bebek Penyet with Rice is an Indonesian dish consisting of deep-fried smashed duck served alongside fragrant steamed white rice, a robust sambal chilli paste, and fresh vegetable accompaniments such as sliced cucumber and tomato. The term 'penyet' derives from the Javanese word meaning 'smashed' or 'pressed', referring to the technique of flattening the marinated duck before or after frying to create a characteristically crispy, shattered skin with tender, juicy meat inside. The duck is typically marinated in a spice paste of shallots, garlic, lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, and coriander before being deep-fried in hot oil. The sambal served alongside is usually a coarse, fiery blend of fresh red chillies, shrimp paste (belacan), lime juice, and salt, providing a sharp, pungent counterpoint to the rich duck. Originating from East Java, Indonesia, bebek penyet has become widely popular across Southeast Asia and is a staple offering at Indonesian-style warungs and restaurants in Singapore. Nutritionally, a standard 450 g plate provides approximately 878 kcal, 48.6 g protein, 32.4 g fat, and 101 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~1395 mg per serving) due to the sambal and seasoning, and carries a moderate glycemic load (~27) driven by the white rice component. It is suitable for halal diets. Clinically, it warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension (high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate glycemic load from rice), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat and cholesterol from fried duck skin). The dish is rich in B vitamins, iron, and zinc from the duck meat.
common_user_queries: How many calories in bebek penyet with rice?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry covers bebek penyet with rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination of FAO/INFOODS SEA fried duck data and USDA SR Legacy cooked white rice data, with minor contributions from sambal and vegetable sides. GI is an analogous estimate anchored to University of Sydney GI Database white rice values. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no single approved-source record covers bebek penyet with rice as a unified dish. Values should be reviewed if an HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA direct entry becomes available. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy sources used for this composite estimate. 3. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source for fried duck, and the composite estimate does not yield a defensible selenium value. 4. trans_fat_g is estimated at 0.1 g per 100 g reflecting minor trans fat from deep-frying oil; this is a conservative estimate. 5. The dish is classified under 'Meat and alternatives' rather than 'Grains and staples' (as in the input seed record) because the defining component is the duck protein preparation; rice is a side component. 6. Halal status is assumed based on the Indonesian Muslim culinary tradition of bebek penyet; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 7. GI set to 68 as an analogous estimate based on white rice GI from University of Sydney GI Database; the fat and protein from duck moderates the overall meal GI slightly below plain white rice (~72). 8. Cuisine is classified as Indonesian (origin) served in Singapore context; the input seed listed 'Singapore' as cuisine which reflects the serving location rather than culinary origin.
